sentences.text: Yeah.
In-- in-- before Rosh-- before the high holidays, New Year's Jewish New Year, there was some rumors that they're going to have a deportation.
In the building where I used to live before was a little house in the back, like a warehouse.
And there were two stores there-- a Jewish hardware store, and a glass-- a-- a-- a warehouse [? from ?] glass.
The owner from this hardware store was still in ghetto, so I told them we can go and hide there.
So we went there.
And there was-- this little house that had these two stores, and they had attic on top.
And from his store, was-- we could take out the floor and there was like a basement, but it wasn't a basement.
It was just like a hole-- an empty hole there.
So we were-- during the [? war-- ?] during the deportation, we went down there.
And one night, we went up to get some water or something.
Margot-- I went out to get some water.
We heard across the street knocking-- [SPEAKING GERMAN]-- this mean out, out, out.
And Margot was ready.
She couldn't take it anymore.
That was already a few days we were there.
So she says, let's go out.
And I said, no.
Hold it.
In the meantime, they were screaming across the street, not to us.
She thought that may be for us.
And that-- we survived there, but we had taken in-- you know that when you hide in the outside of-- of the ghetto, you have to have somebody to give you some information, what's going on.
So we took in a Jewish ordnungsdienst, a wife, and his mother, to this bunker.
And this couple, and my brother, and Margot and I.
So we were there.
And after the deportation came through-- and I was warned from this ordnungsdienst that he doesn't care for his wife, but his mother mean a lot to him.

And he's supposed to come and tell us during the night and take some people to the ghetto, because you couldn't get in the ghetto unless you had somebody taking you in, because the one that was on the gate on ghetto wouldn't let you in without the ordnungsdienst.
So he came one day.
He says, it's over and he'll take his mother and Margot.
And he's going to come the next night.
And I knew he's not going to come anymore.
If he takes out his mother, he's not going to come anymore.
So I said, no.
I didn't want to be stuck with this older couple-- with his wife.
So I says, you take your wife, and you take this couple.
And I will-- my brother and I will stay.
They have to take out their shoes, 2 o'clock in the morning, and walk about a block and a half to the gate from the ghetto to get into the ghetto.
And naturally, he didn't came anymore.
And I was there with my other brother.
It was a day, two days, three days nobody came.
So I says to my brother, the-- the hiding place was behind the city hall.
In [INAUDIBLE]-- in Poland, their lunch time is from 1:00 to 3:00.
There's quiet.
Nobody's there.
And Madrich place was not too far away.
So I says to my brother, I take a chance.
I take my jacket over my armband and I'll go.
If I don't come back, that mean that the place is opened by Madrich.
You come out after me.
And doing good enough, I went.
And Madrich was open.
I went in and then my brother came also.
And we went in-- back to the-- with the group to-- to the ghetto.
The group-- nobody knew that we are working there or not, because we were working there, but at that time, we weren't there.
When I came to this place there was a German.
He looked at me.
I was all gray, because we were sitting there for 10 days in this bunker without food, without anything.
He says, what happened to you?
So I told him.
So he says to me if I want something to eat.
I said, no.
I just want to wash up.
So he gave me permission to go into the bathroom and to wash up.
And my brother came, so we walked back to the ghetto.
That was-- that was called the second-- during-- during-- while we were sitting there, still during the Rosh Hashanah, we had one prayer book.
In order to think, we went up to the attic.
And the attic had little windows in the-- in the-- in the roof.
So some Polish people, kids, were playing around and they were jumping around on this roof.
And they looked at him.
And I thought for sure that he saw us, because he was sitting there-- up there on this roof.
And he-- if he would have seen us, he would have screamed, oh, oh, Jews, Jews!
And this would be the end of it.
But thank God, the sun was hitting this window and it blinded him.
So he couldn't-- he didn't see it.
He saw just-- he looked in, but he couldn't see anything.
So we were safe at that time.
Finally, when we got back to the ghettos.
So we worked there for another-- until September 1943.
On September-- 1st of September, 1943, they said that ghetto will be liquidated.
In the ghetto, there were different times.
There were a time when all the groups has to live in a-- we were moving from one to the other one.
They said, this Madrich building.
So all the people work for Madrich has to live in this building.
The other groups were-- [?
Groschneider ?], I was in this building.
So we lived in a-- in a apartment which was three rooms and a kitchen.
The kitchen was a very small kitchen.
There were maybe 30 or 40 people in this apartment.
So we were married-- new-- newly married.
So we got-- took the kitchen for ourselves.
Little kitchen.
So it was very, very small kitchen.
It's just-- I don't remember if it even had a bed there.
So anyway, we lived there and the rest of the people.
In the morning, when we have to wash up, there were lines waiting for the-- to get into the washroom or to the-- or to the sink to wash up.
That was the living.
And we didn't have any gas.
They didn't permit gas, so what did we do?
There was the meter.
They took-- they locked the meters for the gas.
So I put a piece of rubber-- rubber-- ruber pipe.
Opened up the meters and put it in.
So we-- and everybody-- and somebody has to stay outside and watch it if nobody's coming.
So this is the way we cooked in the-- in the ghetto.
And then we decided to get married.
On July-- on July the 24th, we decided to get married.
It was Margot's birthday.
What year was that?
So we had a rabbi coming.
And we had some people invited for-- not for big thing, just to have-- to have 10, 12 people.
In the meantime, the Gestapo decided to spoil this.
So they lined up about 19 people outside of this building, because they were missing a day of work, and they shot them outside the building.
So that's the way we got married in this-- in the house with five or six people.
My brother Chaim was there, a cousin of mine, and our neighbor, which lived in the same building, and a cousin of Margot was there, I think.
That was the-- the whole ceremony.
That was the wedding.
Then in-- and then was another deportation.
Then when we lived already there, they-- it was-- when was this?
It must have been in-- in August.
It must have been around August 1943.
They decided, again, there were rumors there's gonna be a deportation.
So there was a building in the [?
Agut ?] where my brother lived.
Moishe lived with his wife and his children.
And there was a basement.
It was occupied by the Judenrat for-- for flour for the bread for the-- for the ghetto.
And the one that was in charge of this lived also in the same building.
He had a wife and a small child.
So I went to him and I says, look, I can manage to separate this basement with a wall.

That if you put all these bags with flour against the wall so that you wouldn't see it's a new-- a new wall, if you opened up in a bathroom from-- from our apartment a hole, so we can go into this other basement, we shouldn't have any other entrance.
And at that time, we lined up 73 people.
It was on a Sunday morning.
And my sister-in-law was there with the children.
So I decided we need some milk.
So I ran to the ghetto fence, and there were Polish people came there.
So I bought a bottle of milk.
And when I ran back, the minute I ran back, the SS came into the ghetto.
And that was about a half a block away from the-- from this house.
So I ran in.
And the-- the opening was-- and there was a bathtub.
The bathtub was like-- not like today they have the bathtub.
That was on little legs.
You know, the old fashioned.
So we made rollers on these legs.
And we opened up this.
You get in and pull with a string the bathtub on top of it.
So-- so when I came in, in the last minute, I got into the house.
And I ran down.
And I still was on the stepladder to go down, I heard already the SS coming in.
There were 73 people in this-- this basement.
There was a woman with two daughters.
Her husband left Tarnow for Canada during the war time.
I don't how we got it.
And he made papers for the wife and these two daughters to come there.
If not for this bunker, they would never got there.
Because they were safe in this bunker, they went to Canada and they lived too.
So anyway, that was another day that we were-- I was still on this-- I didn't have time to go down from this stepladder and I heard already the-- they were looking all over the building.
Whoever was in the building, they were taken out.
And at that time, they just-- and then came the-- the-- the September the 1st.
They announced that the ghetto will be liquidated.
We lived in this building from Madrich.
So they said everyone has to go down to the appellplatz.
Very early in the morning, my nephew came.
And he says to me that the ghetto is-- is encircled.
He saw it, because they lived near the-- near the fence and out the window could look out.
So he saw very early in the morning, about 4 or 5 o'clock.
So we came around to me and he told me the ghetto's encircled.
And so-- and then we were ordered to go out, so we went out.
Everybody has to stay with their own group-- working groups.
So I was standing with Margot.
With-- My brother Moishe was working also in the tailoring business, so he went with our group, and his two boys.
And his wife went out with the-- with the A ghetto and she passed by us.
Goeth came.
About 8:00, 9 o'clock, [?
Goeth ?] came with the dogs.
And first of all, the group went out.
So my sister-in-law went by with a little girl.
And she told me, that's all I have.
And then I couldn't do anything, and she left.
Then [?
Goeth ?] saw the two boys, so he started chasing them out.
He chased them out from this side.
They came from the other side.
So that was three times that he took him out.
And he says, if they come back once more, he will shoot them.
So they went out and never saw them again.
And then we went-- we were standing there all day long.
That's was on a Thursday, the 2nd of September.
All day, and the next day Friday afternoon, finally, they took us to the train.
All the other people went out to Auschwitz.
Whatever he didn't like-- they was too old or something.
And Madrich showed up for a few minutes to this appellplatz.
And he saw and talked to [?
Goeth ?] or something and he left, because he probably told him that his people shouldn't be touched.
And Friday afternoon, we went to the train.
In the train, I had a neighbor.
He was a dentist, and he had a little boy, and he had a wife and a daughter.
The wife and daughter went out first day.
This little boy, he had in a backpack with sleeping pills.
He was sleeping there for 36 hours.
Finally, when he came to train, he thought he saved him already.
So I took him out.
And they packed us in 105 people in one car.
And when they took out this little boy, he was-- he didn't know what's going on.
And he gave him an apple to eat.
And he started eating the apple.
And all of a sudden, the Gestapo-- the SS came in.
And they pushed everybody in one side from the car.
And one by one, we have to go through until he find this little boy.
And the people were so anxious to get-- to sit down, to relax a little, so they started pushing him.
So this little boy says, don't push me.
I know I'm gonna die.
And they took out about 30 kids from on the train, because a lot of people-- some of the other people somehow managed to get in the kids.
And about a block away, we had the shooting.
So they shot all this 30 people, the kids there.
And finally, we arrived in Plaszow.
A Friday night, we arrived in Plaszow.
So they took us in in a fancy-- in a fenced place.
It was-- and they had a-- a-- a barrack here, which the Judenrat-- you know what the Judenrat was, right?
Why don't you tell us about it.
The-- the-- in-- in-- in Plaszow was a commandant-- a Jewish commandant.
He had his people.
They overlooked, and they walked hand-in-hand with the-- with the-- with [?
Goeth. ?] So they were sitting in this room.
And everybody has to go by this barrack to go into our own barracks-- has to go there.
In the meantime, they search what you have.
They searched so that even they opened up the lapels to see if you have something sewed in in the lapels.
So they were sitting there.
And naturally, they found a lot of money.
I was still around there.
And didn't go.
I had money with me.
I had a bundle with dollars and with some jewelry.
And I know I cannot go through there, because otherwise, they will take it away.
Somehow, I managed to overhear-- he was another ordnungsdienst.
He wants that nobody should go to here.
But he had an agreement with another ordnungsdienst if he let somebody go through here, he have to pay him off.
So he had a little sign that if you come there, you tell him this and this things, you go through.
I overheard this, so I says to my brother, let's go.
And I came to this door.
And I told him this-- this-- this signal.
So he says, straight, straight, straight.
So we omitted to go there to the search.
And he did know from us anyway, anymore, because he didn't tell us to go through-- by coincidence.
So I had this money with me.
Finally, we got in in a barrack.
They separated the women in a different barrack, the men in a different barrack.
So we-- we separated money too.
We didn't know, if in case of Margot go someplace else.
So she has some money.
So we give her some money, and we had something with us.
One day, Margot was-- didn't feel good.
So she went to the-- she wanted to go to the [SPEAKING GERMAN].
That means like a nurse-- a first aid, overnight.
So she came to me.
And she was afraid to take this along.
So she threw to me.
She was very excited and very nervous.
So she threw it.
So I was afraid to bend down, because we were standing on the appellplatz for the appell.
And that wasn't the Germans there.
There was only the block elders there.
So I took this little bowl between my legs and I stand there.
And then I bent down and I picked it up.
So that was another time that I saved a few-- a few-- some money.
I'm ashamed to say.
Inside in the-- in the body.
And we came-- then we were in Plaszow, so we had a little help, because first of all, the trucks when they came in, we worked in the shipping department.
Shipping department had two different-- you had several advantages.
First of all, there was a night shift and a day shift.
One week these people work night shift, and the other one day shift.
We had a steady day shift, because the shipping department doesn't need a night shift, so that was the first advantage.
The second advantage was the trucks came to pick up the merchandise, they-- and they brought in bread from the-- from Krakow.
So they always had an extra few breads so we could buy from them.
And nobody knew, so we could help.
We helped our relatives there.
We have some friends.
So we always had an advantage.
And the second advantage was the manager from Madrich by the name Titsch-- he was a very nice man.
He was probably an invalid, because he was limping.
So he didn't go to the army so he had this job.
So he went every day to-- to Krakow of a night and came back in the morning.
Nobody checked his trunks in the car-- what he has there.
So one day, he says to Margot-- [SPEAKING GERMAN].
This mean, why do you so pale?
So she says, you know that-- what the conditions we are.
He says, do you have some money?
So she says, yeah.
So he says, give me a little note.
And I'll see-- he had these people in Krakow.
They bought everything on the black market.
So he brought us almost every week one little extra package.
So it was-- this was an advantage, because we could-- we had better-- better nourishment.
Then came-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that was going on like this for-- from September 1943 until-- yeah, until 1944.
The end of October '44.
In the meantime, we were called-- Mengele came.
You know who Mengele is?
The doctor, the-- from Auschwitz.
He wants to make-- in the meantime, they had the kids.
They-- they took all the kids on a-- they had loudspeakers all over.
And they took all the kids there [INAUDIBLE] still to Plaszow.
And they took them out and they had big loudspeakers.
[SPEAKING GERMAN], good night.
And so on and so on, the lolly-- lolly things.
And they took the kids with trucks to Auschwitz.
The mothers were running and screaming.
You saw in the movie this-- this-- this scene.
So that was one day, too.
Then Mengele announced that he's gonna come.
Everybody has to come out and have to go-- that was the 1st of May.
Everybody has to go in naked.
So they it took us out about 6 o'clock in the morning.
The women on one side of the appellplatz, the men on the other side.
Everybody has to get undressed and waiting.
We were waiting a couple hours until Mengele came.
He came in a uniform with a sheepskin coat over his shoulders.
And he had this pencil in his hand.
And they lined up tables with chairs.
And they were sitting there.
And he-- everybody has to go by to him.
And he'd showed here or there, here or there.
I had a hernia.
And I was afraid if he's gonna see the hernia, he's going to send me to the wrong side.
So I put my clothes in my jacket.
And I hold this-- my bundle like this, hanging with the sleeve down so he couldn't see.
So he send me to the right place.
That was another time that I was safe.
And then came the deportation, completely.
They said the Russian front is going forward.
They didn't say this, but we knew.
The Russian front is going forwards, and they are liquidating Plaszow.
So at that time was the time when Schindler-- we didn't know-- we didn't know Schindler at all.
At that time, Schindler's people-- Schindler decided that he's gonna take his people to Czechoslovakia.
And he opened up a factory in Czechoslovakia for ammunition.
And he asked Madrich to come along with his people.
So Madrich, for some reason, didn't go.
I don't know what was his reason.
I don't know.
But anyway, somehow it-- Schindler didn't have enough people that he had permission.
So he took some people from Madrich.
The people from Madrich Titsch gave the list.
So that's-- Margot knew Titsch, so she asked Titsch about and he put her-- put us on the list.
But she put us on the list.
And she came back.
And I said about-- she forgot that my brother's name.
So she went back, and he put my brother's name also.
So in the meantime, they took us not to Brunnlitz.
They took us to Gross-Rosen.
When we came to Gross-Rosen, Gross-Rosen was a camp, it run by criminals-- German criminals.
They were taken out from the criminals, and they gave them.
When we came there, I had still some rings from Margot.
And I didn't know what to do with them.
So there was a brick-- a-- a brick wall.
So I pulled out a brick, and I put these rings.
I counted the bricks from the bottom and from the side, so I'll remember.
When I get back, I'll take them out.
In the meantime, they took us from there.
We left all our clothes.
We have to go to another appellplatz.
We never came back over there.
So on the other appellplatz, they start shaving us.
They shaved us from the top to the bottom.
And then they took us to a-- to a barrack, which was supposed to be a-- a shower.
In the outside, they had bowls with detergent.
They told us everybody should put yourself on with this detergent.
That was burning like-- like terrible.
And then we into the shower, there was no water.
So and then they chased us out from the other side.
And we have to stay and wait during the night-- the middle the night-- until everybody comes out.
Then they took us into the barracks.
And took us into the barracks.
Everything was rush, rush, rush, rush, running, running.
When we came to the barracks, they gave us a pair of shoes, a pair of pants, a jacket, and a nightgown, and a little cap.
The-- the-- the suit that I pulled out a thread, because I wanted to see what kind-- what this is.
So I opened it up and it was newspaper.
They made from newspaper made threads and the threads they made-- it was a-- a-- paper-- paper suit.
My nightgown was too long, so I ripped off a piece and I use it for a-- for a towel.
That was my-- my towel for this.
We were there about several days.
They took us into a room which could hold the most-- the most, 300 people.
They took in 700-800 people.
And we were sitting one between the legs, the other one-- that's the way we slept day and night, day and night.
During the day, they take us out, outside.
They had 50 bowls for all these people for soup.
They give us the soup, and they was standing there with the-- with the whips.
And if you didn't-- you have to-- supposed to drink the soup and handed over the bowl to the next one.
If you didn't do it fast enough, they knocked out the bowl from your hand, and you didn't have anything.
So that's the way we lived for several days.
I don't remember-- I-- we lost completely count, whatever.
Day and night.
It was all day and night the same thing.
Day and night was the same thing.
We didn't know from anything.
So finally, they took us to a train.
In the morning, they give us a soup to eat.
It was grass and water.
Real grass and water-- not cooked even.
Nobody could eat it.
And they give us a sandwich in paper packed, but they told us not to eat this until we get into the train.
Whoever eats it before will be shot.
So regardless how hungry-- how hungry we were, nobody ate anything.
Nobody--
Finally, we go into the train, the minute they locked the doors, everybody grabbed and ate it.
We were traveling I don't remember how long, until we arrived in Brunnlitz.
No.
There was a lot of-- a lot of manipulations on the list.
I didn't know from anything.
Our list from-- from Madrich was OK, they didn't touch.
But there was a guy by the name-- what's his name?
[INAUDIBLE].
I forgot the name.
He-- he got hold of the list, and he start manipulating-- manipulating the list.
Some of them gave him-- he says, whoever had money and things got on the list.
And even if some of them, they paid him off, they still didn't get list-- on the list.
So finally, we-- we arrived in Brunnlitz.
When we came to Brunnlitz, it was a different story.
Brunnlitz, first of all, they had food for us.
We ate.
Then we didn't have any beds, because everything was taken over-- in fact, that was a textile factory from before-- a Jewish factory from before.
He took over this building.
And until everything was there, it took a few days until they had beds, so we slept on the floors.
But there was-- it was very-- better than-- than in Plaszow, because first of all, we won't worry about.
He says, there's no shooting going to be here.
Who said?
Schindler.
Says there's no shooting.
Nobody can came into this camp without his-- his authority.
Whoever wants to come in, has to come into him first.
He-- he dictated over there these things, so SS didn't have too much to say.
That's the way we lived there for seven-- for seven months.
Yeah.
There was just the men.
We didn't know anything about over there.
We thought that they went to Brunnlitz because that's supposed to be-- the next day, they supposed to go to Brunnlitz When we came to Brunnlitz, we heard that they landed in Auschwitz.
But they says every day was some other women that are coming there.
They're coming today, they're coming today.
It was-- took three weeks, or maybe four weeks-- I don't remember how long.
It took several weeks.
We already was in-- in doubt if they're going to come.
But finally, one day they-- in the morning, they said they are coming.
And in the afternoon, they came.
So it was-- it was a relief.
Huh?
I-- we didn't-- they didn't let us out from the factory, but I went on the balcony and I looked.
And I saw somebody-- his wife.
And I says to him, I saw your wife.
And he says, I saw your wife.
So I didn't my wife until a few hours later.
But that was-- that was the condition.
Then we worked there.
Only during lunchtime we could sit together.
A half an hour.
Otherwise, the [INAUDIBLE] were separate-- the women were separate and the men were separate.
We understood that the-- the camp in Plaszow is be liquidated.
So we knew this cannot be good regardless whatever is going to happen to us.
But Schindler's list, when we heard what Schindler was doing in Plaszow, we thought that maybe this is a good chance.
In Plaszow we work for Madrich.
He had several things.
He had the mail factory.
And then he had days when he knew there's going to be a deportation.
He told the people not to go to the-- to the camp, to sleep over there.
And then he took his-- his factory out from-- from camp.
And they took him-- took him in-- every day took them out to-- to his factory.
So [?
Goeth ?] couldn't do so much for-- bad for them, because he took them out in the morning.
Goeth ?] didn't have any-- any things to do with them.
Yeah.
We knew about this.
We knew about Schindler's factories are better conditions.
But we thought that Madrich is also safe, because in a way, it was safe.
But the only thing that Madrich didn't want to risk his money, I guess, to take us to-- to Brunnlitz.
